claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 19 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know you have one child. To help assess your eligibility, I need to ask a few questions.

First, what is your child's name and how old are they?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex, and they're 19 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 19 years old, their eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training, as Child Benefit can continue until age 20 in those circumstances.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in approved education. They're studying for their A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's helpful, thank you. Since Alex is 19 and in approved education (studying A-levels), that's a good sign.

Now I need to ask about your circumstances: Do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand your relationship to Alex and your living situation.

Do you live with Alex, or if not, do you contribute financially to their upkeep (such as paying for food, clothes, or pocket money)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Now I need to check one more important thing:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 19 years old and studying for A-levels at sixth form college, which counts as approved education
- Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 who are in approved education or training
- You live in the UK and Alex lives with you, so you're responsible for them
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

You can go ahead and make a claim for Child Benefit. Just be aware that if you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge, though you can still claim the benefit itself.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and is currently in approved education (studying A-levels at sixth form college). According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives at home with them, meaning they are responsible for the child. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 19-year-old child Alex. Although Alex is over 16, they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which allows Child Benefit to continue. The claimant lives in the UK, lives with Alex, and no one else is claiming for them, meeting all the necessary criteria.

According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. The claimant lives in the UK and Alex lives at home with them, meaning they are responsible for the child. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 19-year-old child Alex. Although Alex is over 16, they are under 20 and in approved education (A-levels at sixth form), which allows Child Benefit to continue. The claimant lives in the UK, lives with Alex, and no one else is claiming for them, meeting all the necessary criteria.
